content:
{
  "title": "Retrieval-Augmented Counterfactual Self-Consistency Prompting for Reducing LLM Hallucinations",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) frequently generate factually incorrect ('hallucinated') outputs, especially when handling complex factual queries. Existing prompting strategies are insufficient to reliably constrain LLM generations to verifiable information.",
  "motivation": "Hallucinations undermine LLM trustworthiness, hindering adoption in domains such as medicine, science, and law. As LLMs are increasingly used as knowledge assistants, effective methods to steer outputs toward factual consistency with external sources are urgently needed.",
  "method": "We propose a novel prompting framework that augments user queries with (1) retrieved evidence snippets and (2) a 'counterfactual challenge' instruction: the model must answer the query and then explicitly identify which claims in its answer could be false based on the evidence. By forcing the model to self-critique its response in context of the evidence, we hypothesize this prompt reduces unsupported assertions and improves factual grounding. This approach is modular and can be applied to any LLM with retrieval access.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on the FEVER and TruthfulQA benchmarks using GPT-4 and Llama-2, comparing our method against Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with standard instructions as the primary baseline. Metrics include factuality (e.g., FEVER label accuracy), hallucination rate (human and automatic), and self-critique usefulness. Statistical significance will be assessed across 500+ queries per dataset."
}
